summary: Adversarial judge panel auditing BoardSmith for arch/security/perf/usability/DX flaws, each finding verified against source
logs: [stall] agent "judge:performance" stalled (no progress) after 720s — 4 StructuredOutput validation failures (last input: {"summary":"The incremental MCTS engine (apply/undo on one game instance, no per-node clone) and the memoized UI enrichment index are well-designed. But the snapshot/checkpoint subsystem scales quadratically and is invisible to designers: GameRunner.actionCheckpoints accumulates one full game.toJSON…) — retrying (1/5)
Done: 31 findings across 5 dimensions; 29 confirmed real by skeptic.

dimensions.summary: BoardSmith's engine is broadly thoughtful — the recent move to a state-authoritative `GameRunner.fromSnapshot` (runner.ts:303) is a genuinely good architectural decision, command/element/flow separation is mostly clean, and error messages are generally actionable. But the persistence/restore layer has accreted into a sprawl of overlapping, partially-contradictory mechanisms that violate single-source-of-truth and, worse, disagree about correctness: the same "rebuild a game" operation is implemented five different ways with three different soundness guarantees, and the debug/time-travel path still uses the very replay mechanism the rest of the system was rewritten to abandon as "unsound." On top of that, the `Game` class is a 2684-LOC god object, declared array state is silently swapped for dev-only Proxies (creating dual sources of truth and dev/prod snapshot divergence), the cross-cutting "whose turn" invariant is represented two ways in nine files, and a shared selection-state-machine type carries a `Set` that silently dies when it crosses the stateless executor boundary. These are exactly the traps that bite a designer or AI agent who trusts the abstraction.

findings.title: Debug time-travel/rewind uses the replay path the codebase itself declares 'unsound' — silently shows WRONG historical state
findings.evidence: src/session/state-history.ts:114 `GameRunner.replay<G>(...)` in getStateAtAction and :428 in rewindToAction. But src/runtime/runner.ts:291-298 says of replay: "The previous replay-based restore was ... also unsound: selection-step / pending-completed actions are recorded in NEITHER command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positioned the flow and crashed real games". The same file's undoToTurnStart (state-history.ts:347-371) and stateless-ops.ts:367-374 both explicitly DON'T replay and restore from checkpoints instead, with the comment: "Replay re-runs start() + recorded actions, which never re-applies pending/selection mutations (Piece.putInto, recorded in neither command nor action history) ... it loses prior-turn equipment and mis-positions the flow". getStateAtAction is exposed live via game-session.ts:772.
findings.whyConcern: Within a single subsystem, undo restores authoritatively from checkpoints while time-travel/diff/rewind replay actions. The replay path is documented elsewhere as producing wrong state and crashing. So the debug panel's time-travel and state-diff can show a board that never actually existed, and rewind can resurrect a corrupted/mis-positioned flow — the exact failure class fromSnapshot was written to eliminate, still live in the debugger.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer or AI agent debugging a misbehaving game trusts the time-travel inspector to show ground truth. Here it can silently lie (lost Piece.putInto mutations, mis-positioned flow), sending them chasing a phantom bug or 'fixing' correct code. The debugger is the one tool that must never lie.
findings.fixPaths: Route getStateAtAction/getStateDiff/rewindToAction through the same checkpoint-based restore (GameRunner.fromSnapshot + actionCheckpoints) that undoToTurnStart and stateless-ops already use; delete the replay calls in state-history.ts. Keep one restore primitive.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified all citations against source. The finding is factually accurate. state-history.ts:114 (getStateAtAction) and :428 (rewindToAction) call GameRunner.replay, while undoToTurnStart (state-history.ts:354-371) and stateless-ops.ts handleUndo (367-390) deliberately AVOID replay and restore authoritatively from actionCheckpoints via fromSnapshot. runner.ts:280-301 explicitly documents replay as "unsound": pending/selection mutations (Piece.putInto) are recorded in neither command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positions the flow and crashed real games (the exact bug class fromSnapshot was written to eliminate). getStateDiff (state-history.ts:166-167) composes two getStateAtAction calls, so it inherits the same unsoundness. These are reachable live: server/handlers/games.ts:248/271/902 and worker/index.ts:1164/1190.

The inconsistency is genuine AND fixable with existing infrastructure: actionCheckpoints[k] (runner.ts:64-112) holds authoritative state at every action index in a live session, so time-travel/rewind could use fromSnapshot(checkpoints[k]) just like undo does. This is a real architecture-level intra-subsystem contradiction.

Impact assessment tempering the severity: (1) These are debug/dev-only features (time-travel debugging, state-diff highlighting, rewind explicitly "intended for debug/development use"). (2) The read paths (getStateAtAction/getStateDiff) are wrapped in try/catch and return {success:false} on throw — they cannot crash the live session; worst case they show wrong/missing historical state. (3) The wrongness only manifests for games that use pending/selection mutations (equipment via Piece.putInto, selection-step mutations); simpler games whose entire state lives in command/action history replay correctly. rewindToAction is the most serious because it replaces the live runner, saves, and broadcasts — for affected games it can corrupt/mis-position the live dev game — but it is still a debug-gated operation, not normal gameplay.

So: real correctness/architecture bug, accurately described ("silently shows WRONG historical state"; rewind can resurrect a mis-positioned flow), but confined to debug/dev tooling with non-destructive, error-caught read paths. That makes "high" slightly overstated; medium is the appropriate independent severity.

findings.title: Snapshot/restore/checkpoint sprawl: 5 restore paths + 2 unrelated checkpoint systems, no single source of truth
findings.evidence: Restore mechanisms: (1) GameRunner.fromSnapshot — state-authoritative (runner.ts:303); (2) GameRunner.replay — action replay (runner.ts:261); (3) captureDevState/restoreDevState — HMR transfer (game-session.ts:#reloadWithDevTransfer ~948-1060); (4) Game.restoreGame/loadSerializedState (game.ts:2660,2612); (5) #reloadFromCheckpoint partial replay (game-session.ts:1132). Two SEPARATE checkpoint systems: runner.actionCheckpoints — per-action, carried inside the snapshot, used for undo (runner.ts:64-111) AND CheckpointManager — 'every N actions, kept in memory, not persisted' for HMR (checkpoint-manager.ts:1-9,57-69). game-session.ts reloadWithCurrentRules (870-936) chains dev-transfer → checkpoint partial-replay → full replay as cascading fallbacks.
findings.whyConcern: There is no canonical answer to 'how is a game reconstructed from saved state.' Each path has different soundness (replay unsound, fromSnapshot authoritative, dev-transfer schema-validated, partial-replay hybrid) and different inputs (action history vs element JSON vs per-action checkpoints vs every-N checkpoints). The two checkpoint systems share the word 'checkpoint' but are unrelated, which is a naming/maintenance trap.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An AI agent or contributor extending persistence cannot know which path is authoritative or which to hook. Adding state (a new field, a new mutation kind) requires correctly updating an unknown subset of five paths; missing one yields a state-loss bug that only surfaces in one mode (undo vs HMR vs time-travel vs stateless executor). This is the systemic fragility behind the recent back-reference and pending-mutation fix commits.
findings.fixPaths: Collapse to one authoritative restore primitive (fromSnapshot) and express undo/rewind/time-travel/HMR as 'produce a snapshot, then fromSnapshot.' Unify the two checkpoint stores or rename one (e.g. runner.undoCheckpoints vs CheckpointManager.hmrCheckpoints). Make replay a derive-snapshot helper, not a parallel restore path.
findings.verifierReasoning: The cited code structures all exist as described, but the "high-severity sprawl / no single source of truth" framing is exaggerated. Verified each citation: (1) fromSnapshot (runner.ts:303) is the authoritative, dominant production path — 15+ uses in stateless-ops.ts, whose header explicitly declares itself "the single source of truth for running one BoardSmith game operation." (2) Game.restoreGame/loadSerializedState (game.ts:2660/2612) are NOT independent restore paths — loadSerializedState is the primitive fromSnapshot calls (runner.ts:336), and restoreGame is a thin static wrapper (MCTS clone). Counting them separately double-counts layers. (3) GameRunner.replay (runner.ts:261) is used by GameSession.restore (game-session.ts:539) only because StoredGameState persists action history, not snapshots — a different storage format that legitimately requires replay — plus time-travel debugging in getStateAtAction (state-history.ts:114). (4) #reloadWithDevTransfer and #reloadFromCheckpoint are dev-only HMR fallbacks forming one deliberate, documented cascade (dev-transfer -> checkpoint partial-replay -> full replay, game-session.ts:970-1000). So there is a clear per-domain hierarchy with documented canonical answers, not undifferentiated sprawl; the comments even explain why replay was abandoned inside fromSnapshot for soundness. The only genuinely fair point is the naming overlap: runner.actionCheckpoints (per-action undo, carried in snapshot, runner.ts:64-111) vs CheckpointManager (every-N, in-memory, dev-only HMR, checkpoint-manager.ts:1-9) are unrelated mechanisms sharing the word "checkpoint" — a minor maintainability nit, mitigated by living in different modules with distinct documented purposes. No correctness bug is demonstrated; the architecture is intentional and heavily commented. Real as an observation, but overstated; effectively a low-severity naming/cleanliness concern.

findings.title: Declared array state is silently replaced by a dev-only Proxy backed by a magic settings key — dual source of truth + dev/prod snapshot divergence
findings.evidence: game.ts:485-488 runs _checkForVolatileState only when `process.env?.NODE_ENV !== 'production'`. That walks own properties and, for any array, calls _autoSyncArray (game.ts:548-551) which via _autoSyncArrayOnTarget (598-721) replaces the property with an Object.defineProperty getter returning a `new Proxy(...)` whose mutations write to `this.settings['__autoSync_'+key]` (623,653,675). Meanwhile toJSON serializes the SAME array as an ordinary attribute (game-element.ts:704-711 iterates all own non-'_' keys). On restore, the proxy prefers the settings copy over the attribute copy: `if (storedData && Array.isArray(storedData)) backingArray = [...storedData]` (game.ts:614-617).
findings.whyConcern: The same array now lives twice in a dev snapshot (attributes.<key> AND settings.__autoSync_<key>) with settings silently winning on restore, and zero times in settings for a production snapshot (the proxy is never installed). The declared property's runtime identity (plain Array vs Proxy) and the serialized snapshot's shape both depend on NODE_ENV.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer writes `items: Card[] = []` and gets, in dev only, a Proxy with different identity/reference semantics and a hidden second copy in settings. Bugs that depend on array identity, or on the attribute-vs-settings copy, reproduce in dev but not prod (or vice versa) — the worst kind of footgun. A snapshot captured in dev and restored in prod (or by the stateless executor) can resolve the array from a different source than the designer expects.
findings.fixPaths: Make persistence environment-independent: either always install the sync wrapper or never (prefer never — persist arrays solely via toJSON attributes). If HMR survival is needed, fold it into the one serialization path rather than a parallel settings shadow. Don't mutate the shape of designer-declared properties at runtime.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified every citation in /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/element/game.ts and game-element.ts. game.ts:485-493 gates _checkForVolatileState behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' via queueMicrotask. game.ts:548-550 routes any own array (except 'messages') to _autoSyncArray. game.ts:589-721 replaces the property with an enumerable Object.defineProperty getter (line 718) returning a Proxy that writes mutations to this.settings['__autoSync_'+key] (623,653,675,683,713). game-element.ts:704-711 serializes all own non-'_' keys, and because the getter is enumerable the array is emitted as a normal attribute; game.ts:2458 ALSO serializes the whole settings object (settings is absent from unserializableAttributes at 370-381). So in dev the same array is in the snapshot twice; in prod the proxy is never installed and it appears once with no __autoSync_ key. game.ts:614-617 prefers the settings copy on proxy install; loadSerializedState (2612-2650) copies settings synchronously and re-applies attributes, then the deferred microtask installs the proxy and the settings copy wins in dev, whereas prod uses the attribute copy. All claimed mechanics are accurate. I downgrade from high to medium because the two copies are always written together within a single environment (proxy syncs to settings on every mutation; serialization reads the same backing array), so normal round-trips do not actually corrupt data. The real harm is dev/prod behavioral divergence (Proxy vs plain Array identity, settings-wins vs attribute-wins restore, and dev-only validation in _validateArrayValue that throws in dev but is silently absent in prod) plus a dual-source-of-truth code smell that contradicts the project's stated single-source-of-truth and dev/prod-parity principles.

findings.title: Game is a 2684-LOC god object spanning ~10 responsibilities and inheriting the full Space container API
findings.evidence: src/engine/element/game.ts is 2684 lines; `export class Game extends Space` (game.ts:286-289). ~88 public/protected methods cover: element registry (getElementById:862, registerElements:825), command exec/undo (execute:885, replayCommands:900, undoLastCommand:918, undoCommands:944), action registry+exec (registerAction:962, getActionExecutor:1027, getSelectionChoices:1035), flow control (setFlow:1499, startFlow:1513, continueFlow:1538, restoreFlowState:1611), RNG (getRandomState:1632,setRandomState:1641), player mgmt (players:1755, nextPlayer:1964, setCurrentPlayer:1923), serialization (toJSON:2445, loadSerializedState:2612, restoreGame:2660), debugging (debugElementTree:1216, debugAllActions:1193), animation buffer (animate:2404, pushAnimationEvent:2414), and the dev-only auto-sync proxy machinery (519-721).
findings.whyConcern: Every concern in the engine routes through one class that designers subclass. Because Game extends Space, a designer's game subclass also exposes the entire container/query surface (create/all/first/...) intermixed with lifecycle and flow APIs, blurring 'what am I supposed to override' vs 'what is internal plumbing.'
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The surface an AI agent must reason about to subclass Game is enormous and mixes public design API with internals (replayCommands, restoreFlowState, _autoSync*). Autocomplete and 'what should I call' guidance is drowned; wrong-but-public methods (e.g. calling restoreFlowState or execute directly) are as discoverable as the intended ones.
findings.fixPaths: Extract cohesive collaborators already implied by the method clusters (FlowController, RandomSource, ActionRegistry, Serializer, DebugInspector) and compose them on Game; keep the designer-facing surface small. Consider not having Game extend Space (use composition for the root container).
findings.verifierReasoning: Every factual claim checks out against the source. src/engine/element/game.ts is exactly 2684 lines (wc -l). `export class Game<G,P> extends Space<G,P>` confirmed at game.ts:286-289. All ~24 cited method locations are accurate to the line: getElementById:862, execute:885, replayCommands:900, undoLastCommand:918, undoCommands:944, registerAction:962, getActionExecutor:1027, getSelectionChoices:1035, debugAllActions:1193, debugElementTree:1216, setFlow:1499, startFlow:1513, continueFlow:1538, restoreFlowState:1611, getRandomState:1632, setRandomState:1641, get players:1755, setCurrentPlayer:1923, nextPlayer:1964, animate:2404, pushAnimationEvent:2415, override toJSON:2445, loadSerializedState:2612, registerElements:825. The dev-only proxy machinery (_checkForVolatileState/createSyncProxy) is real in the 519-721 range. The class does cohabit element-registry, command exec/undo, action registry, flow control, RNG, player mgmt, serialization, debugging, and animation concerns, and via `extends Space` designer subclasses do inherit the full container surface (create/all/first/etc). So the description is accurate, not exaggerated.\n\nHowever, this is a maintainability/architecture observation, not a defect: nothing here causes incorrect behavior, data loss, or security issues. Two points temper the claimed 'medium' severity: (1) `Game extends Space` is an intentional and idiomatic design for board-game engines — the game IS the root container/space, so inheriting the container API is by design, not accidental leakage; (2) the responsibilities are documented with extensive JSDoc and the override surface is signposted (static playerView/PlayerClass, registerElements, setFlow). The strongest legitimate extraction candidate is the dev-only proxy/volatile-state machinery (519-721), which is genuinely orthogonal plumbing. Real concern, but its impact is stylistic/long-term-maintenance rather than functional, so I rate it low.

findings.title: PendingActionState carries a Set across the stateless executor's JSON wire boundary — silently lost, re-firing onSelect
findings.evidence: types.ts:142 `onSelectFired?: Set<number>` on PendingActionState, populated in action.ts:1327-1330 `if (!pendingState.onSelectFired) pendingState.onSelectFired = new Set(); pendingState.onSelectFired.add(...)`. In the long-lived GameSession path this object lives in an in-memory Map (pending-action-manager.ts:62 `#pendingActions: Map<number, PendingActionState>`) so the Set survives. But the stateless executor treats pendingState as a wire envelope: stateless-ops.ts declares itself 'Pure ... no memory between calls' (header lines 7-10), takes pendingState as a `Record<string, unknown> | null` param (executeOp:704) and returns it in OpResult.pendingState (:93, handleSelectionStep:294). JSON.stringify(new Set([1])) === '{}'.
findings.whyConcern: The same type has two incompatible serialization contracts: in-memory (Set OK) vs JSON wire (Set → {}). When pendingState round-trips through the stateless executor, onSelectFired is dropped, so per-selection onSelect callbacks that should fire once re-fire on the next step (double-applied side effects in multi-step selections).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer using a repeating/multi-step selection with an onSelect side effect gets correct behavior in the GameSession but duplicated side effects under the stateless dev/executor host — a heisenbug that depends on which runtime executed the move, with no error raised. The type system actively hides it (the field is typed Set, not flagged non-serializable).
findings.fixPaths: Make PendingActionState fully JSON-serializable: replace `Set<number>` with `number[]` (or a Record<number,true>) for onSelectFired. Add a round-trip assertion/test for pendingState across executeOp so non-serializable members fail loudly.
findings.verifierReasoning: The finding claims onSelectFired (a Set on PendingActionState, types.ts:142) is silently lost when pendingState round-trips through the stateless executor, causing onSelect callbacks to re-fire. This is false; the author missed src/session/pick-handler.ts, which is the explicit serialization boundary. In pick-handler.ts:20-32, serializePendingState does `Array.from(s.onSelectFired)` (Set->array) and deserializePendingState does `new Set(onSelectFired)` (array->Set). The stateless executor's handleSelectionStep (stateless-ops.ts:261-296) does NOT touch the Set directly: it forwards the incoming JSON-wire pendingState (where onSelectFired is already an array) to PickHandler.processSelectionStep (line 280), which deserializes array->Set (pick-handler.ts:93) before the in-memory PendingActionManager runs, then re-serializes Set->array (pick-handler.ts:116) and returns it as step.pendingState. handleSelectionStep returns that serialized array form as OpResult.pendingState (stateless-ops.ts:290). So JSON.stringify never sees a raw Set; onSelectFired survives every wire crossing as an array and is reconstituted into a Set each call. The cancel path is handled identically (pick-handler.ts:137). All other OpResult.pendingState exits are null. The premise of the bug (Set silently becoming {}) does not occur. There is even existing test coverage of the tracking (action.test.ts:1688+).

findings.title: 'Who can act' is represented two ways (awaitingPlayers[] vs currentPlayer) and re-branched in 9 files
findings.evidence: The same if/else appears verbatim in many consumers: stateless-ops.ts selectDueAISeat:171-188 and handleDebugActionTraces:625-636; state-history.ts getActionTraces:281-293; plus the field is consumed across src/ai/mcts-bot.ts, src/engine/element/game.ts, src/engine/utils/snapshot.ts, src/engine/flow/engine.ts, src/session/utils.ts, src/session/ai-controller.ts (grep: 9 non-test files reference awaitingPlayers). Pattern: `if (flowState.awaitingPlayers && awaitingPlayers.length > 0) { find player... } else { use currentPlayer }`.
findings.whyConcern: There is no single canonical 'can seat N act now?' predicate; the simultaneous-vs-sequential turn invariant is re-derived everywhere. Any consumer that checks only currentPlayer (forgetting awaitingPlayers) silently mishandles simultaneous-action phases, and vice versa.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Both library maintainers and AI agents adding new turn-aware logic will naturally reach for currentPlayer and silently break simultaneous turns (or the reverse). The footgun is invisible until a game uses the less-common mode.
findings.fixPaths: Centralize one predicate on FlowState/engine, e.g. `flowState.canAct(seat): boolean` and `flowState.dueSeats(): number[]`, and replace every ad-hoc branch with it. Make currentPlayer/awaitingPlayers internal to the flow engine.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Confirmed by reading the cited code. FlowState carries two representations of "who can act": awaitingPlayers[] (simultaneous) and currentPlayer (sequential), defined at src/engine/flow/types.ts:252, produced by src/engine/flow/engine.ts. The "awaitingPlayers first, else currentPlayer" branch is genuinely re-derived across consumers: src/session/stateless-ops.ts:171-188 and 625-636; src/session/state-history.ts:281-293 (nearly verbatim copy of stateless-ops.ts:625-636, both computing isMyTurn + flowAllowedActions); src/session/utils.ts:28-31 and 369-374; src/session/ai-controller.ts:90-104 and 127-128; src/ai/mcts-bot.ts:744-766/849-886. So the duplication claim holds.

Two corrections to the finding's framing: (1) Canonical predicates DO exist — Game.canPlayerAct() at src/engine/element/game.ts:1682-1694 and isPlayersTurn() at src/session/utils.ts:24-35 — but they are themselves duplicates of each other and only return a boolean, so consumers needing flowAllowedActions re-inline anyway. (2) The 'silent mishandling' risk is theoretical: every consumer I inspected correctly checks awaitingPlayers before falling back to currentPlayer, and several of the 9 files (types.ts, engine.ts, snapshot.ts) are engine internals that define/produce the state rather than re-branch it, so the consumer count is inflated.

Net: a genuine duplication/drift maintainability issue (verbatim-duplicated derivation logic + two redundant boolean predicates + a duplicated AI-seat selector) with no demonstrated correctness bug. The two-representation split is also partly intentional state-machine design. Real but lower impact than medium; rate low.

findings.title: Entire element-diff algorithm is copy-pasted between state-history.ts and stateless-ops.ts
findings.evidence: state-history.ts:181-239 defines getComparableAttrs + collectElements + the added/removed/changed loop. stateless-ops.ts:525-599 defines comparableAttrs + collectElements + the identical loop (same field filtering `key === 'player' || key === 'game' || key.startsWith('_')`, same parentId/attrs comparison). Two near-verbatim implementations of the same diff.
findings.whyConcern: Two copies of a non-trivial algorithm will drift. They already differ cosmetically (function names comparableAttrs vs getComparableAttrs); a future fix to one (e.g. handling a new attribute type) won't reach the other, so the GameSession diff and the stateless-executor diff can report different results for the same two states.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The diff feeds debug highlighting; divergent diffs between the two runtimes make the debugger inconsistent depending on host, undermining trust in the tooling and wasting designer time reconciling discrepancies.
findings.fixPaths: Extract a shared computeElementDiff(fromView, toView) helper (e.g. in session/utils.ts or engine/utils) and call it from both state-history.ts and stateless-ops.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified both cited locations. state-history.ts:181-239 (StateHistoryManager.getStateDiff) defines getComparableAttrs(obj), collectElements(node,map,parentId), and the added/removed/changed loop. src/session/stateless-ops.ts:525-599 (handleDebugStateDiff) defines comparableAttrs(node), collectElements(node,map,parentId), and an identical added/changed/removed loop. The logic is verbatim: identical attribute filtering (`key === 'player' || key === 'game' || key.startsWith('_')`), identical JSON.stringify of the filtered map, identical recursion handling id-bearing vs id-less nodes, and identical diff comparison (`from.parentId !== to.parentId || from.attrs !== to.attrs`). The only differences are cosmetic: function names (getComparableAttrs vs comparableAttrs) and a named DiffElement type alias in stateless-ops. So this is a true copy-paste duplication of a non-trivial algorithm. (Minor evidence error: stateless-ops.ts lives in src/session/, not src/runtime/ as claimed.) Severity: this is a maintainability/drift risk, not a current bug — both copies currently produce identical results, so no functional defect exists today. The drift concern is plausible but speculative, and both copies live in the same module (src/session), making consolidation easy. Low severity is appropriate.

findings.title: serializeValue silently drops circular references and over-deep objects, partially corrupting saved state
findings.evidence: game-element.ts:740-786. On a cycle: `if (visited.has(value)) return undefined` (771-772). On depth>100: `console.warn(...); return undefined` (742-744). toJSON (game-element.ts:704-711) serializes EVERY own enumerable non-'_' property of every element through this, so any designer object graph with a back-reference or shared node loses the offending subtree to `undefined` with at most a console.warn.
findings.whyConcern: Serialization is the foundation of every restore/undo/checkpoint/network path. Silently emitting `undefined` for cyclic or deep state means a snapshot can omit real game state with no thrown error; the loss only manifests later as a wrong board after restore.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer (or AI agent) who stores a plain object holding a reference back to its container, or a moderately nested structure, gets silent data loss on save and a corrupted game on reload — exactly the 'graceful degradation that's hidden, not visible' the project's own CLAUDE.md warns against. There is no actionable error pointing at the offending property.
findings.fixPaths: Fail loud: throw an actionable error naming the property path on cycle/over-depth during serialization (the right path is 'use element children or a serializable shape'), rather than returning undefined. Reserve silent truncation, if any, for a clearly opt-in mode.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified against /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/element/game-element.ts. The code is exactly as described. serializeValue (lines 740-786) returns undefined on depth>100 with only a console.warn (742-744), and returns undefined on cycle detection (771-773) with NO warning at all — completely silent. toJSON (696-711) routes every own enumerable non-'_' attribute of every element through serializeValue, so the loss applies to all designer attributes. GameElement back-references are safely handled as __elementRef/__playerRef (752-768), so the common parent/child cycle is NOT affected — only plain-object/array attribute graphs are at risk, which narrows the blast radius.

Beyond the stated finding there is an even clearer correctness bug: the `visited` set is added to (774) but never removed after a subtree completes, so it accumulates across the whole traversal. This means a non-circular *shared* node (a diamond — same object referenced from two sibling attributes, e.g. attributes = {a: obj, b: obj}) loses the second occurrence to undefined even though there is no cycle at all. That is silent data loss for legitimate, acyclic structures, triggered more easily than a true cycle.

This directly violates the project's explicit 'Fail fast and loud, not silently' / Pit-of-Success principles in CLAUDE.md: foundational serialization can omit real state and emit a structurally-valid-but-wrong snapshot with no thrown error, surfacing only as a wrong board after restore/undo/checkpoint/network sync. The trigger requires designer object graphs with cycles or shared references in non-element attributes, which is uncommon but plausible, so medium severity is appropriate.

dimensions.summary: The BoardSmith engine's per-player state filtering (getPlayerView) and the lobby's host-only logic checks are individually well-written, but the network layer that exposes them has essentially no authentication or authorization model. Player identity is entirely self-asserted: every HTTP handler trusts a `player`/`playerId` field supplied directly in the request, and the Cloudflare worker's WebSocket upgrade derives the player seat from unverified URL query params. As a result, the careful hidden-information filtering and "host only" lobby gates are trivially bypassable over the wire — any client can read another player's private state, act as any player, impersonate the host, and (via always-on debug routes) stack/peek decks, rewind, or wipe games. Separately, the "sandbox" advertised in the worker docs does not exist at runtime: the only runtime guard (validateCode/withLimits) is never invoked, so game code runs with full host privileges and the eslint rules are lint-time only. These are systemic Pit-of-Failure traps: a designer or AI agent who ships a hidden-information game on the provided server/worker will silently ship a cheatable, data-leaking game while believing the framework handled it. The strongest fixes are to (1) bind identity to a server-issued token/session and (2) make every handler derive `player` from that bound identity rather than from the request body/query.

findings.title: HTTP action endpoint trusts client-supplied player identity (no authn) — act as any player
findings.evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:199-216 `handleAction` does `await session.performAction(request.action, request.player, request.args)` — `request.player` comes straight from the POST body. src/session/game-session.ts:813-820 `performAction` only validates range: `if (player < 1 || player > this.#storedState.playerCount) return {... INVALID_PLAYER}` — it never checks the caller IS that player. The worker repeats this: src/worker/index.ts:1105-1118 `#handleAction` reads `const { action, player, args } = body` and calls `performAction(action, player, args)`.
findings.whyConcern: There is no binding between the network caller and the `player` they claim to be. A `curl -X POST /games/<id>/action -d '{"action":"...","player":2,"args":{...}}'` submits moves on behalf of player 2. The WebSocket path correctly uses the server-tracked `session.playerSeat` (core.ts:505-509), but the HTTP route — shipped in both the standalone server and the production worker — bypasses that entirely.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer/AI agent who builds a game and deploys it on the provided server or worker gets an authoritative-looking server that is actually trivially cheatable: opponents can play your turns, force passes, or grief. The framework gives the impression of enforcing turns/seats while the easy HTTP path silently ignores identity. Wrong usage is the default.
findings.fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleAction), src/worker/index.ts (#handleAction). Derive `player` from an authenticated session/token bound at connect/create time (e.g. a per-seat secret returned from claim-seat/join), not from request.player. Reject if the bound identity != requested player.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed in source. src/server/handlers/games.ts:209 — `handleAction` calls `session.performAction(request.action, request.player, request.args)` where `request.player` is taken directly from the POST body (route wired at core.ts:152-155). src/session/game-session.ts:813-820 — `performAction` only validates the player is in range (`player < 1 || player > playerCount`), with no binding to the network caller. The worker repeats the pattern: src/worker/index.ts:1115-1118 reads `const { action, player, args } = body` and passes `player` straight to `performAction` (and the public worker route at 415-431 forwards the raw body to the Durable Object). By contrast the WebSocket path at core.ts:505-509 correctly uses the server-tracked `session.playerSeat`, never a client-supplied value. I grepped src/server and src/worker for auth/token/bearer/cookie middleware and found none — there is no authentication layer wrapping these HTTP routes, so a `curl -X POST /games/<id>/action -d '{\"action\":...,\"player\":2,...}'` does submit moves as player 2. The vulnerability is real and shipped in both the standalone server and the production worker.\n\nSeverity: This is a genuine authorization/impersonation bypass — any client can act as any seat over HTTP. In the context of a board-game framework the concrete impact is cheating/impersonation (manipulating game outcomes), not RCE or PII exfiltration, and the codebase appears to lack a broader authn model generally (even WS seat assignment via matchmaking findIndex on a client-provided playerId is not strongly verified). Still, the HTTP path is strictly worse because the actor identity is fully client-controlled per request while the WS path at least anchors to a server-tracked seat. I rate it high rather than critical: it is a clear, real authz gap in production code, but its blast radius is game integrity within an active-development library rather than system/data compromise.

findings.title: Hidden-information leak: GET state accepts arbitrary ?player= and returns that player's private view
findings.evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:179-194 `handleGetGame(store, gameId, playerPosition)` uses `const player = playerPosition ?? 1; const result = session.getState(player)`; the value is parsed unauthenticated from the query in core.ts:147 `query.player ? parseInt(query.player, 10) : undefined`. getState -> buildPlayerState -> src/session/utils.ts:360 `const playerView = runner.getPlayerView(playerPosition)` which produces that player's privileged (hidden-info-revealing) view. Worker: src/worker/index.ts:591-594 + #handleGetState(player). The same unauthenticated player param feeds state-at (core.ts:170), state-diff (core.ts:180), pending-action (core.ts:225), action-traces (core.ts:189), and selection-choices (core.ts:281-287).
findings.whyConcern: The engine's per-player filtering is correct, but the transport lets anyone pick whose perspective to render. `GET /games/<id>?player=2` returns player 2's hand/hidden cards to any caller. This defeats hidden information for every card/secret game over HTTP.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Hidden-information is the single hardest thing to get right in a digital board game, and the framework's whole value proposition is doing it for you. A game author reasonably assumes 'the server only sends me my own view.' Instead, any spectator/opponent can read everyone's secrets with a query string. Silent, invisible, catastrophic for game integrity.
findings.fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleGetGame/handleGetStateAt/handleGetStateDiff/handleGetPendingAction/handleGetActionTraces/handleGetSelectionChoices) and src/worker/index.ts equivalents: derive playerPosition from authenticated identity; spectators get position 0 (public view) only.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified at the cited locations (paths corrected: handlers in src/server/handlers/games.ts, routing in src/server/core.ts, not core.ts under handlers). The claim is factually accurate.

- src/server/core.ts:147 parses `query.player` straight from the unauthenticated query string and passes it to handleGetGame. The same untrusted param feeds state-at (170), state-diff (180), action-traces (189), pending-action (225), and selection-choices reads `player` from the POST body (281-287).
- src/server/handlers/games.ts:190-191 `const player = playerPosition ?? 1; const result = session.getState(player)` — no check that the caller is that player.
- session.getState -> buildPlayerState (src/session/utils.ts:351-361) calls `runner.getPlayerView(playerPosition)`, which is the player-specific privileged view (the truthView includes that player's hidden info). The engine filtering is per the requested position, so requesting player=2 returns player 2's private view.
- src/worker/index.ts:591-594 has the identical pattern for the Cloudflare deployment target.

Critically, I searched for any auth layer (Authorization/Bearer/token/secret/player verification) across src/server/*.ts and src/worker/index.ts and found NONE. The server trusts client-supplied player identity everywhere — handleAction (core.ts:209) likewise forwards request.player unverified, so a caller can also act AS any player, not just read their view. So the hidden-info leak is one facet of a complete absence of an authentication/authorization model on the transport.

So `GET /games/<id>?player=2` does return player 2's hand/hidden cards to any caller. This genuinely defeats hidden information for any card/secret game served over HTTP.

Severity: I rate it high rather than critical. It is a real unauthenticated information-disclosure that breaks the core hidden-information guarantee, which would be critical for any publicly exposed deployment. I temper to high because (a) it reflects an across-the-board missing-auth posture (the action endpoint trusts client identity identically) rather than a single broken filter, consistent with an in-development library not yet shown to be deployed to untrusted users, and (b) the action-traces route even carries a comment acknowledging it's a debug endpoint. The fix is architectural: introduce per-player session/token auth so the transport binds the caller to a verified seat instead of trusting the query param. If this server is intended to be reachable by untrusted players as-is, critical is fully defensible.

findings.title: WebSocket identity in the worker is self-asserted via URL params; playerId is also broadcast in lobby state
findings.evidence: src/worker/index.ts:864-894 `#handleWebSocketUpgrade`: `const playerParam = url.searchParams.get('player'); const playerId = url.searchParams.get('playerId'); let playerPosition = playerParam ? parseInt(playerParam,10) : -1;` then if a playerId is given it is mapped via `getSeatForPlayer(playerId)` — but with no playerId at all it just trusts `?player=2`. There is no secret/token check. Meanwhile playerId is leaked to everyone: src/session/lobby-manager.ts:101-128 `getLobbyInfo()` returns `slots` directly, and slots carry `playerId` (set at lobby-manager.ts:196 `slot.playerId = playerId`). getLobbyInfo is broadcast to all clients (lobby-manager.ts:1014-1030) and returned by the lobby GET route.
findings.whyConcern: Two independent impersonation paths: (1) connect with `?player=N` and you are seated as N with their private view and turn rights; (2) read the lobby (which echoes every player's playerId), then reconnect with `?playerId=<victim>` to be mapped to their seat. playerId is treated as a capability/secret elsewhere (host checks) yet is published to all clients.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The production deployment target (Cloudflare worker) is the one most authors will actually ship on. It looks like it has a seat/identity system, but the identity is forgeable and the 'secret' is broadcast. An AI agent wiring up multiplayer will produce a fully impersonable game by default.
findings.fixPaths: src/worker/index.ts (#handleWebSocketUpgrade) and src/session/lobby-manager.ts (getLobbyInfo). Issue an unguessable per-seat token at claim/join and require it on WS connect + every mutating call; strip playerId (and any secret) from broadcast LobbyInfo, sending only a boolean 'isYou'/seat to each recipient.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Confirmed at all cited locations. In src/worker/index.ts:864-894 (`#handleWebSocketUpgrade`), identity is entirely self-asserted: `playerParam`/`?player=N` sets `playerPosition` directly (line 868) with no verification; `?playerId=X` is mapped to a seat via `getSeatForPlayer` (line 877) or `storedState.playerIds.indexOf` (line 882) with no secret/token check. A grep for token/secret/authoriz/authenticat in src/worker/index.ts returns nothing — there is no auth layer. The initial state sent back uses `getState(playerPosition)` (line 917), so an attacker passing `?player=N` receives victim N's private/hidden view and is seated with their turn rights. This is a genuine impersonation path.

The leak path is also real: LobbySlot carries `playerId` (src/types/protocol.ts:44), `getLobbyInfo()` returns `slots` directly plus `creatorId` (lobby-manager.ts:113-127), and `broadcastLobby()` sends the full LobbyInfo to every connected session (lobby-manager.ts:1014-1030). So every client learns every other player's playerId, including the creator's.

Crucially, playerId IS used as a capability elsewhere: host-only operations compare `playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId` (lobby-manager.ts:412, 465, 540, 745, 884) for add/remove/toggle slots, kick, and modify others' options. Since creatorId is broadcast to all clients, any client can replay it to take over host privileges (kick players, reassign roles/colors).

Net effect: anonymous clients can (1) read any player's hidden game state and take their turns via `?player=N`/`?playerId=<victim>`, and (2) seize host control via the broadcast creatorId. This fully breaks the hidden-information and turn-integrity guarantees that are the entire point of a board-game engine. It is a real, present authorization bypass, not exaggerated. I rate it high rather than critical because this is a library-layer concern (a hosting platform could in principle wrap it with auth, and there is no PII/RCE), but as shipped the engine itself performs zero identity verification.

findings.title: Lobby 'host only' authorization is bypassable: caller asserts its own playerId and creatorId is public
findings.evidence: Host gates compare a client-supplied id to creatorId: src/session/lobby-manager.ts:412 `if (playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId) return {... 'Only the host can add slots'}` (same pattern at :465 removeSlot, :540 setSlotAI, :745 kickPlayer, :884 updateSlotPlayerOptions, :942 updateGameOptions). The playerId arrives unauthenticated from the body (e.g. core.ts:346 `const { playerId } = body` for add-slot; worker/index.ts:1330-1345 likewise). creatorId is handed to every client: lobby-manager.ts:120 `creatorId: this.#storedState.creatorId` inside getLobbyInfo.
findings.whyConcern: Any client reads the lobby, learns creatorId, then sends add-slot/remove-slot/set-slot-ai/kick-player/update-game-options with `playerId: <creatorId>` and passes the host check. The authorization is real in code but rests on an unverified, publicly-broadcast identifier.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Designers see explicit 'host only' guards and assume the lobby is safe. In reality any participant can kick others, flip seats to AI, or rewrite game options before start. The guard creates false confidence — a classic footgun.
findings.fixPaths: src/session/lobby-manager.ts (all host-gated methods) + the handlers in src/server/core.ts and src/worker/index.ts that pass body.playerId. Bind host identity to the authenticated session token; remove creatorId from broadcast LobbyInfo.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited locations in the actual source. Host-only authorization is implemented as a plain comparison of a client-supplied playerId against a publicly-broadcast creatorId. (1) Host gates: lobby-manager.ts:412 (addSlot), :465 (removeSlot), and "Verify caller is the host" checks at :744 (kickPlayer), :883 (updateSlotPlayerOptions), :941 (updateGameOptions) all do `playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId`. (2) playerId is unauthenticated: core.ts:346/354/362/378/386/394/402 destructure playerId straight from the request body; the WS path uses session.playerId (types.ts:252, an optional field) which is likewise never verified against any credential. A grep across src/session and src/server for token/secret/jwt/authenticate/verify/cookie/Authorization returned nothing — there is no identity verification layer at all. (3) creatorId is leaked to every client: lobby-manager.ts:120 returns it inside getLobbyInfo(). (4) playerId is client-chosen: joinLobby (lobby-manager.ts:251) accepts any playerId the caller sends. Therefore any participant can read the lobby, copy creatorId, and submit it as their own playerId to pass every host check — enabling kicking players, flipping slots to AI, and mutating game options. The bypass is trivially exploitable. The root cause is actually broader: the whole identity model trusts client-asserted playerId, so players can also impersonate each other on non-host endpoints (setReady, updateName, leaveSeat). The finding is genuine and correctly described.

findings.title: Debug/integrity endpoints (deck stacking, shuffle, rewind, full history) are always wired, no guard
findings.evidence: src/server/core.ts routes these unconditionally: :242 move-to-top, :251 reorder-card, :259 transfer-card, :267 shuffle-deck, :231 rewind, plus :160 history and :167 state-at. Handlers (src/server/handlers/games.ts:922-1020) call session.moveCardToTop/reorderCard/transferCard/shuffleDeck with cardId/deckId straight from the body and no auth. The code even acknowledges the risk but does nothing: core.ts:187 `// Note: This is a debug endpoint. Consider disabling in production.` There is no NODE_ENV/feature-flag gate around any of them.
findings.whyConcern: Any unauthenticated client can move a specific card to the top of a deck, transfer cards between decks, reshuffle, rewind the game to a prior action (discarding others' moves), or dump the full action history (revealing hidden info via replay). This is total game-integrity compromise, not just a leak.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: These are presented as 'debug only' but ship enabled in the shared server. A designer running the provided server in front of real players exposes deck-stacking and rewind to anyone who knows the URL pattern. The safe default would be off-in-production; instead the dangerous default is on.
findings.fixPaths: src/server/core.ts: gate the /debug/* , /rewind, and arguably /history & /state-at routes behind an explicit opt-in (e.g. options.enableDebugRoutes, default false, or NODE_ENV!=='production'); return 404 otherwise.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited lines. src/server/core.ts:229-271 unconditionally wires the rewind and four debug deck-manipulation routes (move-to-top, reorder-card, transfer-card, shuffle-deck); :158-191 unconditionally wire /history and /state-at, and :187 carries the acknowledged-but-unenforced "Consider disabling in production" comment. src/server/handlers/games.ts:922-1020 pass cardId/deckId/targetIndex straight from the request body into session.moveCardToTop/reorderCard/transferCard/shuffleDeck with zero auth; handleGetHistory returns the full history with no player-based redaction. The capabilities are genuinely integrity/confidentiality destroying (stack a deck, transfer cards, reshuffle, rewind to discard others' moves, dump full history). The class even holds #environment (core.ts:106/113) but uses it only for the health check (core.ts:463), never to gate these routes, so a clean fix (gate on environment==='development') is trivially available — a real Pit-of-Success/security flaw in a public library API. However the claimed "high / any unauthenticated client can compromise any production game" overstates current exposure: the Cloudflare worker (src/worker/index.ts) has its own router that does NOT forward the debug/rewind routes and never calls GameServerCore.handleRequest, and the CLI dev server uses its own Vite+WS server. No shipped deployment path currently mounts these endpoints — GameServerCore is the platform-agnostic public router downstream adapters would use. Additionally the entire server layer has no auth model at all (even /action is unauthenticated), so these debug routes aren't a uniquely-unauthenticated escalation but part of a layer assuming an external trust boundary. Real and worth fixing (gate behind environment), but exploitability today is limited, so high is somewhat inflated.

findings.title: Advertised 'sandbox' has no runtime enforcement — game code runs with full host privileges
findings.evidence: src/worker/index.ts:4 documents 'running board games in a sandboxed environment.' The only runtime guard, validateCode (src/engine/sandbox/index.ts:197-223) and withLimits/ExecutionContext, is never called anywhere except a re-export: grep across src shows only src/engine/index.ts:254-260 references (ExecutionContext, withLimits, withLimitsAsync, validateCode, DEFAULT_LIMITS) — no call site in runtime/session/server. performAction (game-session.ts:822) calls runner.performAction with no limits wrapper. Even if used, validateCode is a regex string-scan (e.g. `/\bfetch\s*\(/`) trivially defeated by `globalThis['fet'+'ch']`. eslint-plugin rules are lint-time only.
findings.whyConcern: Game definitions are ordinary imported JS modules executed in-process. They can call fetch, fs, eval, setTimeout, Math.random, read process.env/secrets, or loop forever (no action timeout is enforced). The 'no-network/no-eval/no-timers/no-nondeterministic' guarantees exist only as advisory lint, not isolation.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The framework markets sandboxing and ships eslint rules that imply enforcement, leading authors (and AI agents importing third-party game packages) to trust untrusted game code. The easy path (just import a game) is the unsafe path; there is no hard boundary. Determinism guarantees (critical for replay/undo correctness) are also unenforced at runtime, so Math.random/Date.now in a game silently corrupts replays.
findings.fixPaths: Either wire ExecutionContext/withLimitsAsync around runner.performAction (src/runtime + src/session/game-session.ts) and run game code in a real isolate (worker/V8 isolate, vm with frozen globals), or stop advertising 'sandbox' and document that game code is fully trusted. Remove/clarify the dead validateCode if not enforced.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim checks out against the source:

1. The sandbox module (src/engine/sandbox/index.ts) defines ExecutionContext, withLimits, withLimitsAsync, guard, DEFAULT_LIMITS, and validateCode. A repo-wide grep (excluding tests) shows these symbols appear ONLY at their definition site and as a pure re-export in src/engine/index.ts:254-260. There is no call site in runtime/session/server/worker. Confirmed dead code.

2. validateCode (lines 197-223) is indeed a naive regex string-scan (e.g. /\bfetch\s*\(/, /\beval\s*\(/, /\bMath\.random\s*\(/). It is trivially bypassable via dynamic property access (globalThis['fet'+'ch']) and is never invoked anyway.

3. The action execution path has no limits/timeout wrapper: GameSession.performAction (game-session.ts:822) calls this.#runner.performAction directly, and runner.performAction (runner.ts:117-) executes game logic with no ExecutionContext, no actionTimeout, no operation counter. So actionTimeout/maxOperations/maxRecursionDepth in DEFAULT_LIMITS are never enforced. An infinite loop in game code would hang the host.

4. src/worker/index.ts:4 literally says "running board games in a sandboxed environment" — but there is no node:vm / isolate / worker_thread isolation anywhere in worker or server (grep found none). The eslint-plugin rules are lint-time only.

So the gap is genuinely present: the "sandbox" is advisory lint plus an unwired, regex-based module. However, I down-rate the severity from the claimed 'high' for the security category. BoardSmith's deployment model loads game definitions as build-time bundled modules (buildRegistries([gameDefinition]) passed by the deployer), not as untrusted, user-uploaded runtime code. There is no evidence of an arbitrary-game-upload/marketplace path that would feed attacker-controlled code into the engine. Under the trusted-developer model, this is not a privilege-escalation vulnerability — the developer already controls the deployment. The eslint rules (no-network/no-timers/no-nondeterministic) primarily protect event-sourced replay determinism (a correctness property), and the missing action timeout is a DoS/robustness gap. The real, present problems are: (a) a misleading "sandboxed" claim that over-promises isolation, (b) dead sandbox code that should be wired up or removed (Pit of Success / no-fallbacks violation), and (c) no runtime timeout protecting against runaway game logic. These are worth fixing but do not constitute a high-severity exploitable security hole in the current architecture. Hence medium.

findings.title: Unauthenticated restart wipes the game; create/action have no rate limiting or game-count cap (DoS)
findings.evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:330-369 handleRestart takes only gameId, calls `store.deleteGame(gameId)` then recreates — no player/host check; routed at core.ts:299-303 with no identity. Worker mirrors this (worker/index.ts:635-639, #handleRestart at 1236). No rate limiting exists on any route (no middleware in core.ts handleRequest:119-471). createGame (handlers/games.ts:72) has no per-caller cap; the in-memory store grows unbounded.
findings.whyConcern: Any client can POST /games/<id>/restart to destroy an in-progress game's state for all players (data loss). With no rate limiting, an attacker can spam /games to exhaust memory (in-memory/SQLite stores) or flood /action. WS messages are JSON.parsed (core.ts via handlers, worker/index.ts:948) with no size/type schema validation.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A deployed game has a one-request griefing button (restart) and no abuse controls out of the box. Authors won't expect 'restart' to be an unauthenticated reset-for-everyone, nor that the reference server has zero DoS protection — they'll discover it in production.
findings.fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleRestart: require host/authenticated identity), src/server/core.ts (add rate limiting + max-games cap, validate WS message shape/size), src/worker/index.ts (#handleRestart auth).
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified all cited locations. handleRestart (src/server/handlers/games.ts:330-369) accepts only (store, registry, gameId, aiConfig) — no player/host/creator identity param — and unconditionally calls `store.deleteGame(gameId)` then `store.createGame` to rebuild from storedState. The route (src/server/core.ts:299-303) passes only the path-matched gameId, so any client that knows/guesses a gameId can destroy an in-progress game's state for all players. handleCreateGame (games.ts:72-174) generates a fresh gameId on every POST /games with no per-caller cap, and grep confirms NO rate-limiting code exists anywhere in src/server or src/worker. So the DoS (unbounded memory growth via /games spam, /action flooding) and data-loss (unauthenticated restart) claims are both genuinely present in the code.

Important context that shapes severity: this is not a restart-specific gap — the ENTIRE server has no authentication/authorization model. /action, /undo, the debug deck-manipulation routes (move-to-top, shuffle, transfer), and rewind are all equally unauthenticated, and lobby endpoints trust a self-asserted playerId from the body rather than any verified token. So an attacker can already submit moves as any player via /action, which is at least as damaging as restart. The finding correctly identifies a real, systemic trust-the-client design rather than an isolated bug. Impact is griefing/data-loss within game sessions and memory exhaustion — no sensitive-data exposure, and this is an actively-developed library where deployment-layer auth may be expected. Medium is the appropriate severity.

findings.title: Internal error messages leaked to clients, violating the project's own 'never leak implementation details' rule
findings.evidence: src/server/core.ts:75-78 `serverError` returns `error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal error'` directly in the HTTP body for any uncaught exception (catch at :467-470 `console.error('Server error:', error); return serverError(error)`). Worker does the same: src/worker/index.ts:741-746 returns `error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal error'`. CLAUDE.md error-handling rules explicitly say 'Never leak implementation details (line numbers, stack traces, internal paths).'
findings.whyConcern: Engine/internal exceptions (which often embed element ids, property paths, or class names) are surfaced verbatim to any caller, aiding reconnaissance and potentially leaking game-internal structure or hidden-state hints.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The framework's stated secure-error posture is contradicted by its own reference server, so authors inherit a leak they were told the framework avoids. Low severity but a credibility/trap issue given the explicit rule.
findings.fixPaths: src/server/core.ts (serverError) and src/worker/index.ts catch block: log full error server-side, return a generic message + error id to clients; only surface validated, user-actionable messages.
findings.verifierReasoning: Confirmed in source. src/server/core.ts:75-78 `serverError` returns `error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal error'` directly as the HTTP body, and the top-level catch at :467-470 logs then calls `serverError(error)` for ANY uncaught exception. The worker mirrors this exactly at src/worker/index.ts:741-746, returning `error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal error'` to the client. So arbitrary engine/internal exception messages are surfaced verbatim to any caller. This does violate the project's own CLAUDE.md error-handling rule: 'Never leak implementation details (line numbers, stack traces, internal paths)' and 'Error messages should be as descriptive as possible for users' — the intent is curated user-facing messages, not raw exception text. Caveat that tempers severity: only `error.message` is returned, not the stack trace, file paths, or line numbers (those go to console.error server-side only). Engine validation errors are often intentionally descriptive and safe, but uncaught exceptions can embed element ids, property paths, or class names. No credentials or auth bypass involved; impact is information disclosure / reconnaissance aid. Low severity is correct — a genuine but minor hygiene/info-leak issue, not a high-impact vuln.

dimensions.summary: Incremental designs are mostly sound; the undo/checkpoint subsystem and MCTS are the main performance liabilities (3 high-severity findings below).

findings.title: Per-action undo checkpoints accumulate unbounded full snapshots (O(N^2) memory)
findings.evidence: runner.ts:69 actionCheckpoints never pruned; captureCheckpoint (107-112) keeps entries 0..len-1 forever, one per action. Each is a full createSnapshot (snapshot.ts:108-123): state=game.toJSON plus a copy of game.commandHistory (game.ts:891 only pushes, never trims). game-session.ts:1464 calls it on EVERY broadcast.
findings.whyConcern: Long games hold hundreds of full deep state copies in memory; in a ~128MB Durable Object kept for the game lifetime this is an OOM/GC path, plus a full toJSON per action.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer builds a normal multi-turn game and memory grows superlinearly with no warning or knob; the right path is the failing path.
findings.fixPaths: /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/runtime/runner.ts; /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/utils/snapshot.ts
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified every cited location. runner.ts:107-112 captureCheckpoint() does actionCheckpoints.slice(0,len) then sets [len], freezing [0..len-1] and growing one full snapshot per recorded action; actionHistory is whole-game (only trimmed on undo). snapshot.ts:111-122 createSnapshot copies state=game.toJSON() AND commandHistory=[...game.commandHistory] per entry. game.ts:885-895 execute() only pushes to commandHistory (the sole pop at 931 is MCTS undoLastCommand, not session play), so it grows O(N) over a game; copying it into each of N checkpoints gives O(N^2) total command-entry copies. game-session.ts:1464 calls captureCheckpoint() on every broadcast (the universal post-mutation funnel), so a full toJSON runs per action. Crucially, undo only ever consumes the current turn's checkpoint (state-history.ts:354-369; stateless-ops.ts:476-482) and slices to turnStartActionIndex+1 only after an undo — checkpoints below the current turn start are never pruned during forward play, so they are pure dead weight retained for the game lifetime in the in-memory runner. This is genuine unbounded accumulation: O(N) full state snapshots plus O(N^2) command-history copies, plus a full serialize per broadcast. The fix (cap retention at current turn start) is simple. The 'OOM' framing is slightly strong for short games but the scaling defect and avoidable cost are real, and heavy long-lived games (which they explicitly target) make it material.

findings.title: Stateless op snapshot embeds the entire checkpoint array, persisted per op (O(N^2))
findings.evidence: runner.ts:224-228 getSnapshot returns a snapshot carrying all N full per-action checkpoints. Each op calls fromSnapshot (runner.ts:330) then returns runner.getSnapshot (stateless-ops.ts:251), which the caller persists and passes to the next op. Each op reads N states and writes N+1.
findings.whyConcern: Per-op payload and storage grow linearly with actions; O(N^2) total. KV/object-store value-size limits eventually hard-fail a game mid-session.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Every game runs this path; an agent stores OpResult.snapshot verbatim and silently gets quadratic storage growth and a size-limit time bomb.
findings.fixPaths: /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/runtime/runner.ts; /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/session/stateless-ops.ts
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified at all cited locations. GameRunner.getSnapshot (runner.ts:224-228) returns the base snapshot plus a copy of actionCheckpoints. captureCheckpoint (runner.ts:107-112) only does slice(0, len) and sets [len] — it never prunes older checkpoints, so a game with N actions permanently carries N+1 checkpoints. Each checkpoint is a FULL snapshot from createSnapshot (snapshot.ts:103-123) embedding state: game.toJSON() (entire element tree) plus copies of commandHistory and actionHistory; snapshot.ts:52-62 confirms entries are non-nested but full-state. Every mutating handler in stateless-ops.ts (224/268/304/332/352/404) calls fromSnapshot then returns runner.getSnapshot (251/209/445 and via stateEnvelope at 142); executeOp is documented (700-705) as pure with the caller persisting OpResult.snapshot and passing it to the next op. Therefore each persisted snapshot is O(N) full game states (actually O(N·S + N²) since checkpoint[k] also carries k actions), and cumulative write volume across a game is O(N²·S). This is unmitigated; full retention serves authoritative undo (handleUndo 375-390) and dev-only debug time-travel (runnerFromCheckpoint 471-485). The hard-fail concern is real: per-snapshot size grows linearly with full game states, and Durable Object storage caps values at 128 KiB, so a long game could fail mid-session. Minor overstatement in the evidence: fromSnapshot performs one read of an object containing N states, not N separate reads — but the O(N) embedded-state-per-op and O(N²) total substance is correct.

findings.title: MCTS incremental undo silently degrades to a full game rebuild per iteration for card games
findings.evidence: undoCommands rolls back each iteration (mcts-bot.ts:662,690); on failure recoverFromUndoFailure (1253-1258) does restoreGame (new GameClass + loadSerializedState + restoreFlowState, 1214-1247). inverse.ts returns null for SHUFFLE(70-72), CREATE(58-65), REMOVE(67), SET_VISIBILITY(74-79), so undo fails and backprop recovers and returns (663-671).
findings.whyConcern: Card games (Go Fish, Cribbage) deal, shuffle, flip, discard constantly, so most playouts force a full reconstruction every iteration, 10-100x slower, blowing the search timeout or Worker CPU; the advertised incremental design (24-31) reverts to reconstruct-per-iteration.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An agent generating a card-game bot via generate-ai gets correct but mysteriously slow or timing-out MCTS with no warning naming non-invertible commands.
findings.fixPaths: /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/ai/mcts-bot.ts; /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/command/inverse.ts
findings.verifierReasoning:
The underlying mechanism exists. undoCommands (game.ts:944-949) stops and returns false on the first non-invertible command (via undoLastCommand, which fails when _inverseHistory entry is null, lines 921-925). createInverseCommand (inverse.ts:50-113) returns null for SHUFFLE (70-72), CREATE (58-61), CREATE_MANY (63-65), ADD_VISIBLE_TO (77-79), START_GAME, END_GAME. On undo failure, backpropagateWithUndo (mcts-bot.ts:662-671, 690-697) calls recoverFromUndoFailure (1253-1258), which does a full restoreGame (new GameClass + loadSerializedState + restoreFlowState, 1214-1247). So a playout that crosses a SHUFFLE does force a full rebuild that iteration, defeating the incremental design. That much is genuinely present.

However, the finding's evidence is substantially WRONG, which inflates its severity:
1. The claim asserts REMOVE returns null at line 67. FALSE — inverse.ts:67-68 is `case 'REMOVE': return createRemoveInverse(game, command);`, which returns a valid MOVE inverse (164-182). Discards/removes are invertible.
2. The claim asserts SET_VISIBILITY returns null at 74-79. FALSE — inverse.ts:74-75 is `case 'SET_VISIBILITY': return createSetVisibilityInverse(...)`, a valid inverse (188-200). The author conflated it with ADD_VISIBLE_TO (77-79). Card flips are invertible.
3. MOVE (draw/deal of an existing card) is invertible (119-136).

So the most common card operations the title leans on — deal, flip, discard, draw — are all invertible. The only realistic mid-playout trigger is SHUFFLE (CREATE happens at setup, before the search root, not during playouts). SHUFFLE during a playout only occurs at reshuffle / new-round deals, which for a depth-capped playout (playoutDepth, mcts-bot.ts:572) may not even be reached. So "most playouts force a full reconstruction every iteration" is unsupported.

Impact is also bounded: when recovery fires, the cost is one restoreGame per affected iteration — essentially equivalent to the clone-per-iteration baseline that non-incremental MCTS uses anyway. The "10-100x slower" figure is unsubstantiated; this is graceful degradation to baseline, not catastrophic. The recovery path is also functionally correct (state-authoritative restore + rootCommandCount reset), so there is no correctness bug.

Real mechanism, but the supporting facts are partly incorrect and the severity/frequency are exaggerated. Genuine but minor — a lost optimization for shuffle-heavy multi-round games, not a high-severity defect.

dimensions.summary: BoardSmith's authoring surface is largely thoughtful — it has guard rails (devWarn for loop safety, ElementCollection.includes/indexOf warnings steering to contains(), DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS runtime cap so loops can't truly hang, an unknown-action typo warning in the flow engine, and many "PIT OF SUCCESS"-labeled auto-unwrap/auto-fill behaviors in useActionController). The ActionPanel uses native <button> elements throughout, which gives keyboard/focus accessibility for free. However, there are genuine footguns that undermine the Pit-of-Success goal for both human designers and AI agents: the single most-used authoring API (the action builder's execute handler) provides zero type linkage from declared selections to handler args, forcing manual `as` casts on every game; a prominent error message points users to a method that does not exist (`game.defineAction`); the board-interaction composable that a HARD RULE depends on fails silently when missing; and the element-matching logic uses OR-semantics across id/name/notation that can select the wrong element. There is also confusing overlap between three element-selection builder methods. None of these are catastrophic data-loss bugs, but several are classic traps where the easy path silently does the wrong thing.

findings.title: Action execute() args are Record<string, unknown> — no type inference from declared selections, forcing manual casts on every action
findings.evidence: action-builder.ts:554-556 `execute(fn: (args: Record<string, unknown>, context: ActionContext) => ActionResult | void)` and types.ts:436 `execute: (args: Record<string, unknown>, context: ActionContext) => ...`. Each chooseFrom/chooseElement/fromElements is generic over <T> but the type is discarded (returns `this`, not an accumulated arg map). Real game code proves the cost: checkers/src/rules/actions.ts:172-173 `const piece = args.piece as CheckerPiece; const destination = args.destination as DestinationChoice;`. The builder docstrings show `.execute(({ target }) => target.takeDamage(10))` as if `target` were typed, but it is `unknown`.
findings.whyConcern: The most frequently used authoring API gives no type safety between the selection name/type declared in the builder and the args received in execute(). A wrong cast (e.g. `args.piece as Square`) compiles cleanly and crashes at runtime; a renamed/typo'd selection key (`args.peice`) is `unknown` with no error. This directly violates the CLAUDE.md goal 'impossible to represent invalid states in types.'
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The easy path (destructure args) yields `unknown`, so the path of least resistance is to sprinkle `as` casts that defeat the compiler. AI agents generating games will produce casts that may not match the declared selection types, creating silent runtime bugs the type system was supposed to catch. There is no compile-time signal when a selection is added/renamed but the execute body isn't updated.
findings.fixPaths: Accumulate an args type through the fluent chain: make each builder method return `Action<Args & { [name]: T }>` so execute() infers a fully-typed args object. Standard TS builder pattern; would remove every `as` cast in execute bodies.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified at the cited locations. action/action-builder.ts:554-556 declares execute as `fn: (args: Record<string, unknown>, context: ActionContext) => ActionResult | void`, and action/types.ts:436 has the same `Record<string, unknown>` signature. The builder selection methods (chooseFrom<T> line 193, chooseElement<T> line 280, fromElements<T> line 369) are generic over T but all return `this` (the Action class), so the declared selection name/type is never accumulated into a typed arg map for execute — the generic is used only to type the internal Selection config. The docstrings (lines 277, 544-551) show `.execute(({ target }) => target.takeDamage(10))` as if `target` were typed, but at the type level args is `unknown`. Real game code confirms the cost: BoardSmithGames/checkers/src/rules/actions.ts:172-173 does `const piece = args.piece as CheckerPiece; const destination = args.destination as DestinationChoice;`. A typo'd key or wrong cast compiles cleanly and would fail at runtime, which conflicts with the CLAUDE.md 'impossible to represent invalid states in types' goal. Downgrading from high to medium: this is an ergonomics/type-safety gap on the most-used authoring API, not a correctness or security bug — authored code runs correctly, casts are explicit, and the builder-returns-this pattern is a common, non-breaking tradeoff.

findings.title: Unknown-action flow warning points to a non-existent method game.defineAction()
findings.evidence: flow/engine.ts:1224-1227 and 1300-1303 both emit: `references unknown action '${actionName}'. Did you forget to register it with game.defineAction('${actionName}', ...)?`. But `defineAction` does not exist anywhere in src (grep finds it ONLY in these two strings). The real API is `registerAction(action: ActionDefinition)` / `registerActions(...)` (game.ts:962, 991), which takes an ActionDefinition object — not `(name, ...)`.
findings.whyConcern: CLAUDE.md requires error messages to be 'actionable' and to give 'clear error messages with actionable next steps.' This message sends the user to a method that does not exist and even shows a wrong signature, so following the guidance produces a second error ('game.defineAction is not a function').
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer or AI agent who makes an action-name typo in a flow step hits this warning, then writes `game.defineAction('foo', ...)` as instructed and gets a fresh failure — compounding the confusion at exactly the moment they're already stuck. The advice actively leads them deeper into the pit.
findings.fixPaths: Change both strings to reference the real API, e.g. `Register it via this.registerAction(create${'<Name>'}Action(this)) inside your game's setup (see registerActions()).` flow/engine.ts:1226 and :1302.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified at src/engine/flow/engine.ts:1226 and :1302 — both console.warn calls say `game.defineAction('${actionName}', ...)`. grep confirms `defineAction` (singular) appears ONLY in these two strings; it is not a real method. The actual API in src/engine/element/game.ts is `registerAction(action: ActionDefinition)` (line 962) and `registerActions(...actions: ActionDefinition[])` (line 991), both object-based, not `(name, ...)`. There is a `defineActions()` (plural) override hook (line 257) but no singular `defineAction`. So the message names a non-existent method and shows a wrong signature — following it yields a second error. The finding is genuine and contradicts CLAUDE.md's 'actionable error messages' rule. Severity downgraded to low: it is a console.warn (no crash/broken behavior), emitted only when a flow already references an unknown action, and the developer would quickly find the correct method. It is a wording defect in a diagnostic string, not a high-impact bug.

findings.title: useBoardInteraction()/injectBoardInteraction() return undefined silently — the HARD-RULE interaction channel fails with no diagnostic
findings.evidence: useBoardInteraction.ts:393-395 `export function useBoardInteraction(): BoardInteraction | undefined { return inject(BOARD_INTERACTION_KEY); }` and useActionController.ts:1576-1578 `injectBoardInteraction()` just forwards it. Contrast with the sibling injectActionController.ts:1507-1512 which DOES throw: `if (!controller) { throw new Error('injectActionController() must be called inside a GameShell context'); }`. Real game UIs are forced to litter guards: hex/src/ui/components/HexBoard.vue:101 `if (!boardInteraction) return false;` and checkers/src/ui/components/CheckersBoard.vue repeatedly `if (boardInteraction) {...}` / `boardInteraction?.clear()`.
findings.whyConcern: A HARD RULE states 'All UI interactions must work in a Custom UI and Action Panel in parity ... through useBoardInteraction.' Yet the entry point to that channel returns undefined when used outside a GameShell/provider, so highlighting, selection, and drag-drop all silently no-op (every isSelectable/isHighlighted returns false) with zero error to explain why. The inconsistency with injectActionController (which throws) means two sibling APIs have opposite failure modes.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: The easy path — call useBoardInteraction() and use it — silently breaks the exact feature the hard rule mandates if the component isn't mounted under GameShell. Designers/agents must defensively add `?.`/early-return guards everywhere (and forgetting one yields a raw 'Cannot read properties of undefined' instead of a guided message). Wrong setup is invisible rather than loud.
findings.fixPaths: Provide a throwing variant matching injectActionController (e.g. injectBoardInteraction throws an actionable 'must be used inside <GameShell>' error), and keep an explicit `tryUseBoardInteraction()` for the optional case. useBoardInteraction.ts:393, useActionController.ts:1576.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited code. useBoardInteraction.ts:393-395 returns inject(BOARD_INTERACTION_KEY) with no default/throw, so it yields undefined silently outside a provider. injectBoardInteraction (useActionController.ts:1576-1578) merely forwards it. The sibling injectActionController (useActionController.ts:1507-1513) DOES throw 'injectActionController() must be called inside a GameShell context' — confirming two sibling injection APIs have opposite failure modes. Game UIs do litter guards: hex HexBoard.vue:101 `if (!boardInteraction) return false;` and checkers CheckersBoard.vue uses `boardInteraction?.clear()` / `if (boardInteraction)` at lines 376, 397, 433, 440, 447, 454, 580. So the inconsistency and silent-undefined behavior are factually real. However the severity is somewhat overstated: GameShell.vue:444 always calls provideBoardInteraction, so in correct usage (inside GameShell) the channel never fails — undefined only occurs on misuse outside a GameShell. The HARD RULE about parity is not actually broken for shipping games; the real cost is defensive-guard boilerplate plus a missing diagnostic on misuse. That's a genuine DX/API-consistency problem worth fixing (mirror injectActionController by throwing, or provide a sensible default), but it does not break correctly-used code, so low rather than medium.

findings.title: Element matching uses OR across id/name/notation — can select/highlight the WRONG element when names or notations collide
