findings.evidence: useBoardInteraction.ts:206-211 `matchesRef`: `if (ref.id !== undefined && element.id === ref.id) return true; if (ref.name && element.name === ref.name) return true; if (ref.notation && element.notation === ref.notation) return true;`. Because branches are independent ORs, a ref that carries BOTH id and name (e.g. boardRef returns `{ id: 5, name: 'Militia' }`) will match a DIFFERENT element with id 8 but name 'Militia' via the name branch. triggerElementSelect (line 279-285) then fires `state.onElementSelect(validElem.id)` using the ref's id (5), so clicking piece 8 selects piece 5.
findings.whyConcern: Identifier precedence is silently wrong: when a precise id is present it should win, and name/notation should only be fallbacks when id is absent. As written, any duplicate-named elements (very common: 'Militia', 'Pawn', 'Worker') create cross-talk between board clicks/highlights and the action being built.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: A designer who follows the docs and returns a rich `{ id, name }` boardRef (more info = better, they'd assume) actually introduces a selection bug that's intermittent and hard to trace — the board highlights/acts on the wrong piece only when names collide. The 'more descriptive' path is the buggy path.
findings.fixPaths: Make matchesRef precedence-based: if ref.id !== undefined, match by id ONLY; else fall back to notation, then name. useBoardInteraction.ts:206-211.
findings.verifierReasoning:
The cited code is accurate. `matchesRef` (useBoardInteraction.ts:206-211) is a flat OR across id/name/notation with no precedence: `if (ref.id !== undefined && element.id === ref.id) return true; if (ref.name && element.name === ref.name) return true; if (ref.notation && element.notation === ref.notation) return true;`. It is used by isHighlighted, isSelected, isValidTarget, isSelectableElement, isDisabledElement, triggerElementSelect (line 281), and isDropTarget — so a false match affects selection, highlight, disabled, and drop logic. triggerElementSelect uses `state.validElements.find(ve => matchesRef(element, ve.ref))` then fires `state.onElementSelect(validElem.id)`, so a name/notation match on a ref belonging to a different element does cause selection of the wrong element id.

The claim's two premises both hold in this codebase: (1) duplicate names are a normal, common pattern — `createMany` (game-element.ts:328-342) assigns the SAME `name` to every element it creates, and names are not enforced unique (game-element.ts:53, just an optional field). (2) boardRef returning BOTH id and name is a real, used pattern: BoardSmithGames/polyhedral-potions/src/rules/actions.ts:272,285,380 literally do `boardRef: (die) => ({ id: die.id, name: die.name })`. With such a ref plus duplicate names, the name branch matches a different-id element, and find() returns the wrong validElem (or the first colliding one), so id is silently not authoritative — exactly the described cross-talk.

Caveats reducing severity: the specific example is partly hypothetical. No shipping example game actually triggers it — polyhedral-potions uses {id,name} refs but its 7 dice all have unique names ('d4','d6',...), so no collision occurs; the default ref (pick-handler.ts:387-390) is {id} or {id,notation} and never adds name. The bug requires an author to opt into a multi-identifier ref where a non-id identifier collides. So it is a genuine latent logic/Pit-of-Success defect (a natural ref shape becomes a footgun, id should win when present) but is conditional and not currently producing wrong behavior in any existing game.

findings.title: Three overlapping element-selection builder methods (chooseFrom / chooseElement / fromElements) with contradictory 'prefer X' guidance
findings.evidence: action-builder.ts:113-118 chooseFrom doc: 'For selecting game elements, prefer fromElements instead.' chooseElement doc:216-221 'Uses the board for selection... For choices shown as buttons/dropdowns, prefer fromElements instead.' fromElements doc:301-305 'This is the "pit of success" method for element-based choices.' Internally fromElements single-select even delegates to the SAME 'element' selection type as chooseElement (lines 391-409), so chooseElement(with elements) and fromElements(single) are near-duplicates.
findings.whyConcern: Two of the three methods select elements with substantial overlap, and the guidance both pushes away from chooseElement and toward fromElements while chooseElement itself still accepts a precomputed `elements` array. There is no single obvious 'right' method, and the distinction (board-click vs buttons) isn't enforced by the method you pick — both produce the same selection type.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Designers and especially AI agents must make a non-obvious choice among three similar methods, and the docs contradict each other about which is canonical. This is decision-paralysis / inconsistent-codebase risk: different actions in the same game will use different methods for the same purpose, and agents may pick the deprecated-in-spirit chooseElement.
findings.fixPaths: Collapse to one canonical element-selection method (fromElements) and make chooseElement either a thin documented alias or remove it (CLAUDE.md: 'No Backward Compatibility ... remove the bad thing'); ensure docstrings agree on the single recommended path. action-builder.ts:254-412.
findings.verifierReasoning:
Verified at /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/action/action-builder.ts. The three cited doc comments exist verbatim: chooseFrom (line 115) says prefer fromElements for game elements; chooseElement (lines 219-220) says prefer fromElements for button/dropdown choices; fromElements (line 304) calls itself the "pit of success" method. And fromElements single-select does delegate to the same internal type:'element' selection as chooseElement (lines 391-409). So the surface-level facts hold and there is genuine API-surface redundancy with somewhat contradictory "prefer X" guidance — confirmed by usage showing chooseElement is still the more-used method (26 vs 16 fromElements in src; 9 vs 4 in example games) despite docs steering away from it.

However, the finding's central claim is overstated and partly wrong. It asserts "chooseElement itself still accepts a precomputed `elements` array," making it a "near-duplicate" of fromElements. The public chooseElement options object (action-builder.ts:256-279) has NO `elements` field — it only exposes elementClass/from/filter (board-derived candidates). The `elements` property exists only on the internal ElementSelection type (types.ts:223) and is set exclusively by fromElements. So the two methods take genuinely different inputs (board class/filter vs. an explicit precomputed array) and converge only at the internal selection-type level. That split is a deliberate design choice, not accidental duplication. Likewise, lumping chooseFrom (the value/string/number selector) in as a third "element-selection method" is a stretch.

Net: a real but minor documentation/API-clarity usability nit (redundant element-selection paths + mildly contradictory guidance), not the structural duplication with functional impact the finding implies. No bug, no incorrect behavior. Severity adjusted down from medium to low.

findings.title: Flow variables and forEach loop bindings use untyped magic-string keys (ctx.get/set, forEach `as`) — typos silently return undefined
findings.evidence: flow/types.ts:62-64 `set: (name: string, value: unknown) => void; get: <T = unknown>(name: string) => T | undefined;`. builders.ts:446-454 setVar stores by string; builders.ts:178-193 forEach takes `as: string` and the bound value is later read via ctx.get(as). Example builders.ts:443 `setVar('turnCount', (ctx) => (ctx.get('turnCount') ?? 0) + 1)`.
findings.whyConcern: There is no compile-time relationship between a setVar/forEach key and the ctx.get('key') that reads it. A typo (`ctx.get('turnCout')`) silently returns undefined and the `?? 0` fallback masks it, so a counter silently resets every turn with no error — exactly the 'fallbacks masking real problems' anti-pattern CLAUDE.md warns about.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Designers/agents treat flow vars like normal variables but get none of the safety: misspelled keys, type drift (stored number read as something else via generic <T>), and missing keys all fail silently rather than loudly. The easy path (string keys) has no guard.
findings.fixPaths: Consider a typed flow-state generic on FlowContext (e.g. FlowContext<G, Vars>) or at least a dev warning when get() is called for a key that was never set. flow/types.ts:54-64, flow/builders.ts:446-454.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited locations. flow/types.ts:62-64 defines set/get with bare `string` keys and no type linkage. builders.ts:178-193 forEach uses `as: string`; builders.ts:446-454 setVar stores via ctx.set(name,...) with a string name; builders.ts:443 example uses `(ctx.get('turnCount') ?? 0) + 1`. The described failure mode is genuine: there is no compile-time relationship between a written key and a read key, so a typo'd ctx.get returns undefined, and a `?? default` fallback (idiomatic for counters) silently masks it instead of failing loud — which is exactly the anti-pattern CLAUDE.md warns about. However, impact is minor: the `?? 0` in the example is itself legitimate (the var truly doesn't exist on first iteration), so this is an API-shape/type-safety weakness rather than an active bug, and stringly-typed variable stores are a common, accepted pattern. Real, but low severity as claimed.

dimensions.summary: BoardSmith has genuinely strong loud-failure ergonomics in several places (the `fromJSON` "Unknown element class" error, the flow infinite-loop guard with a stack trace, the `dependentFilter` crash message, and `boardsmith validate`'s determinism scan are all excellent, actionable, pit-of-success error messages). The getting-started doc is accurate and the action/flow builder APIs are mostly discoverable. However, the single most important audience — an AI agent turning a one-line idea into a working game — is undermined by one structural type-safety hole and a cluster of toolchain/doc inconsistencies. The fluent action builder throws away all type information: `ctx.game` is `Game` (defaulted to `any`) and every `args.*` is `unknown`, so the primary API an agent writes (action handlers) is a wall of unchecked `as` casts in every real example game — a silent-mismatch trap. The sandbox guardrails (the eslint-plugin) are dead code never wired into scaffolded projects (nor dogfooded by the repo), with their enforcement re-implemented three separate, inconsistent times. And wrong package import paths (`@boardsmith/engine`, `@boardsmith/ai`, etc.) are baked into runtime "fix-it" error messages, the AI code generator's output, and the slash-command templates an agent follows — so generated code won't resolve and the suggested fixes mislead. These are concrete, fixable issues that directly steer agents into traps.

findings.title: Action handlers are completely untyped: ctx.game is Game<any> and every arg is unknown, forcing unchecked casts everywhere
findings.evidence: The single most-written API for an agent — the action `.execute()` handler — has zero type safety. `ActionContext` is non-generic: `src/engine/action/types.ts:368` `game: Game;` and `:372` `args: Record<string, unknown>;`. `Game` itself defaults its generics to `any`: `src/engine/element/game.ts:286-288` `export class Game<G extends Game = any, P extends Player = any>`. The builder's execute signature discards all accumulated selection types: `src/engine/action/action-builder.ts:554-556` `execute(fn: (args: Record<string, unknown>, context: ActionContext) => ActionResult | void)` — even though `chooseFrom<T>`, `chooseElement<T>` capture `T`, that `T` never threads into `args`. There is no generic `ActionContext<G>` anywhere (grep confirms only one non-generic declaration). The result in EVERY real game: `~/BoardSmithGames/cribbage/src/rules/actions.ts:20` `const game = ctx.game as CribbageGame;` (repeated in ~10 actions), `:98` `const card = args.card as Card;`, `~/BoardSmithGames/checkers/src/rules/actions.ts:172-173` `const piece = args.piece as CheckerPiece;` / `args.destination as DestinationChoice;`. The project's own MEMORY.md records this as a known papercut: "ctx.game in action .execute() callbacks has type Game<any,any> — cast to specific game class".
findings.whyConcern: Casts (`as`) are unchecked assertions. If an agent renames a selection, changes its type, or typos an arg name, TypeScript stays silent and the bug surfaces only at runtime (or as `undefined` propagating into game logic). The library's own motto is 'impossible to represent invalid states in types' — here the opposite is true: the type system actively certifies wrong code as correct.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: This is the deepest trap for AI agents. Agents rely heavily on the type checker as a correctness oracle while iterating; here it provides none for the core game-logic surface. An agent writing `args.card as Card` when the selection is actually named `cards` (plural) gets a clean compile and a runtime `undefined`. Every example game models the cast-everything pattern, so agents are actively trained to bypass type safety.
findings.fixPaths: Make `ActionContext` generic (`ActionContext<G extends Game = Game>`) and make the `Action`/builder generic over an accumulating args record so `chooseFrom<T>('card', ...)` adds `{ card: T }` to the execute args type. Thread the game type from `Game<G>` into `registerAction`/`Action.create<G>()`. At minimum, type `args` per-selection so `execute(({ card }) => ...)` infers `card`'s type. Files: src/engine/action/types.ts, src/engine/action/action-builder.ts, src/engine/element/game.ts (registerAction signature).
findings.verifierReasoning: All cited locations verified in source. types.ts:366-373: ActionContext is a non-generic interface with `game: Game` and `args: Record<string, unknown>`. game.ts:286-289: `class Game<G extends Game = any, P extends Player = any>` — both generics default to `any`. action-builder.ts:554-556: execute() takes `(args: Record<string, unknown>, context: ActionContext)`. The builder `class Action` (line 47) is non-generic and `chooseFrom<T>`/`chooseElement<T>` (lines 153, 254) capture T only locally — there is no accumulated type parameter, so selection types provably cannot reach `args`. grep confirms a single non-generic `ActionContext` declaration and no `ActionContext<...>` usages. The finding's claim that action handlers are untyped and force `as` casts is genuinely correct, and MEMORY.md corroborates it as a known papercut. I downgrade severity: this is a DX/type-safety ergonomics gap affecting game-author code, not a defect causing incorrect library behavior, crashes, data loss, or security exposure. It is pervasive (touches every action) and contradicts the library's stated typing philosophy, which keeps it meaningful, but a documented cast workaround exists and nothing misbehaves at runtime in the library itself. \"Critical\" is inflated; medium is the honest independent rating.

findings.title: Sandbox eslint-plugin is dead code — never wired into scaffolded games, not dogfooded, with a legacy config format and three duplicate enforcement paths
findings.evidence: The eslint-plugin defines the sandbox guardrails (`src/eslint-plugin/index.ts:14-19`: no-network, no-filesystem, no-timers, no-nondeterministic, no-eval). But the scaffold never emits an eslint config or dependency: `src/cli/lib/project-scaffold.ts:472-482` lists every generated file (boardsmith.json, package.json, tsconfig, vite, etc.) — no `.eslintrc`/`eslint.config.*` — and `generatePackageJson` produces scripts `{dev, build, test, validate}` with devDeps `{@vitejs/plugin-vue, typescript, vitest}` — no eslint, no lint script. The repo doesn't even dogfood it: `eslint.config.mjs:2` imports `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin`, not the boardsmith plugin (grep finds zero references to the plugin's rules outside its own dir). Its `configs.recommended` (`src/eslint-plugin/index.ts:23-31`) uses the legacy `.eslintrc` shape `plugins: ['boardsmith']`, which is incompatible with ESLint 9 flat config. Enforcement is instead reimplemented twice as regex: `src/cli/commands/validate.ts:189-198` (network/fs/timers/determinism/eval) and `src/cli/commands/lint.ts` (element-comparison bugs only). The two regex sets don't even agree on coverage.
findings.whyConcern: The whole point of the sandbox rules is to keep agents from writing nondeterministic/networked/timer code that silently breaks replays, MCTS cloning, and multiplayer sync. If they never run, they protect no one. The AST-based plugin (accurate) is abandoned in favor of weaker regex duplicates (false positives/negatives, naive comment stripping).
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An agent scaffolding via `boardsmith init` gets no editor/CI signal when it writes `Math.random()` or `fetch()` in a UI helper. It only learns of a problem if it happens to run `boardsmith validate`, which most agents won't unless told. The guardrail that should make the wrong path hard is invisible.
findings.fixPaths: Pick ONE enforcement path. Wire the eslint-plugin into the scaffold (emit eslint.config.js using flat-config, add eslint + the plugin to devDeps, add a `lint` script), convert `configs.recommended` to flat-config shape, and delete the duplicate regex scanners in validate.ts/lint.ts (or have them delegate to eslint). Files: src/cli/lib/project-scaffold.ts, src/eslint-plugin/index.ts, src/cli/commands/validate.ts, src/cli/commands/lint.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited locations. eslint-plugin/index.ts:13-19 defines the 5 AST sandbox rules; index.ts:22-31 uses the legacy .eslintrc shape (plugins: ['boardsmith']) which is incompatible with the flat-config array the repo actually uses in eslint.config.mjs. project-scaffold.ts generateScaffoldFiles (470-484) emits no eslint config, and generatePackageJson (121) emits scripts {dev,build,test,validate} and devDeps {@vitejs/plugin-vue,typescript,vitest} — no eslint, no lint script. eslint.config.mjs:2 uses @typescript-eslint, not the boardsmith plugin; the only out-of-dir reference is pack.ts:48 naming it as a package string — zero rule usage. validate.ts:189-200 reimplements the same 5 concepts as regex with naive comment stripping (210-217). So the plugin is genuinely dead code: exported but never consumed, never scaffolded, never dogfooded, legacy config format. Two corrections temper the framing: (1) The 'protect no one' claim is overstated — the regex enforcement IS wired into `boardsmith validate` (validate.ts:41) and `validate` IS a scaffolded package.json script, so scaffolded games do get sandbox enforcement, just via the weaker regex. (2) 'Three duplicate enforcement paths' is loose — lint.ts covers a different category (element-comparison bugs), not the sandbox rules, so only two paths cover the sandbox concerns. Net: real DX/dead-code debt with a weaker live regex replacing an accurate abandoned AST plugin, but not a high-severity security gap since enforcement still runs.

findings.title: Wrong package import paths (@boardsmith/engine, @boardsmith/ai, @boardsmith/ui ...) baked into AI codegen, runtime error messages, and slash-command templates
findings.evidence: The published package is `boardsmith` with subpath exports (`package.json`: `.` → engine, `./ai`, `./ui`, `./testing`, etc.). There is no `@boardsmith/*` scope. Yet the AI code generator emits unresolvable imports: `src/ai-trainer/code-generator.ts:54-55` `lines.push(\`import type { Game } from '@boardsmith/engine';\`)` and `import type { Objective } from '@boardsmith/ai';`. The actionable runtime 'fix' message hands the agent a broken path: `src/engine/action/helpers.ts:43` `import { dependentFilter } from '@boardsmith/engine';` (correct is `boardsmith`). The slash-command template an agent follows repeats it: `src/cli/slash-command/generate-ai-instructions.md:112-113`. And ~23 source files carry `from '@boardsmith/ui'` / `@boardsmith/session` / `@boardsmith/testing` JSDoc examples (e.g. `src/ui/composables/useDragDrop.ts:11`, `src/testing/index.ts:18`) that surface via hover/autocomplete.
findings.whyConcern: These aren't comments an agent ignores — the codegen produces files that won't compile, and the error message explicitly tells the agent the wrong way to fix a crash. The getting-started doc uses the correct `boardsmith` / `boardsmith/ui`, so the codebase contradicts itself.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An agent that runs AI generation, hits the dependentFilter error, or reads composable JSDoc will copy `@boardsmith/engine`, get a module-not-found, and waste a debugging cycle on an error the library itself introduced. Generated code is broken out of the box.
findings.fixPaths: Global replace `@boardsmith/engine` → `boardsmith`, `@boardsmith/ai` → `boardsmith/ai`, `@boardsmith/ui` → `boardsmith/ui`, `@boardsmith/session` → `boardsmith/session`, `@boardsmith/testing` → `boardsmith/testing`, `@boardsmith/worker` → `boardsmith/worker`, etc. Priority: src/ai-trainer/code-generator.ts:54-55, src/engine/action/helpers.ts:43, src/cli/slash-command/generate-ai-instructions.md:112-113, then JSDoc across src/ui/composables/*, src/session/*, src/testing/*, src/client/*.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified against source. package.json name is `boardsmith` with subpath exports (`.`→engine, `./ai`, `./ui`, `./session`, `./testing`, etc.); there is no `@boardsmith/*` scope. The cited locations all emit the nonexistent scope: src/ai-trainer/code-generator.ts:54-55 emits `import type { Game } from '@boardsmith/engine'` and `import type { Objective } from '@boardsmith/ai'` (correct: `boardsmith` and `boardsmith/ai`) — these are baked into generated AI eval files that will fail to resolve/compile. src/engine/action/helpers.ts:43 puts `import { dependentFilter } from '@boardsmith/engine'` inside an actionable runtime error message, actively telling the developer the wrong fix. src/cli/slash-command/generate-ai-instructions.md:112-113 repeats it in the template an agent follows. Meanwhile docs (docs/ai-system.md:56,84,180; docs/common-pitfalls.md:510,853,1488) correctly use `boardsmith`/`boardsmith/ai`/`boardsmith/ui`/`boardsmith/testing`, so the codebase contradicts itself. The codegen and error-message instances are load-bearing (broken compile output + misdirection at crash time), not ignorable comments. High is justified for the codegen+error-message impact; the ~23 JSDoc-example occurrences are lower impact but reinforce the inconsistency.

findings.title: Three overlapping element-selection methods (chooseFrom / chooseElement / fromElements) with no obvious 'right' choice
findings.evidence: The builder exposes `chooseFrom<T>` (`src/engine/action/action-builder.ts:153`), `chooseElement<T extends GameElement>` (`:254`), and `fromElements<T extends GameElement>` (`:323`) — plus `enterText`/`enterNumber`. `chooseElement` and `fromElements` both produce single-element selections (fromElements branches to ElementSelection vs ElementsSelection internally) but differ subtly in value encoding — `fromElements`' JSDoc (`:305-307`) stresses 'Custom UIs send the element ID directly', implying `chooseElement` differs. The naming mixes three verb conventions (`choose*`, `from*`, `enter*`) and inconsistent plurality (`chooseElement` singular, `fromElements` plural-but-often-single). Real games pick inconsistently for the same concept: checkers uses `chooseElement<CheckerPiece>('piece')`; cribbage/go-fish use `fromElements<Card>('card')`; polyhedral mixes `chooseElement<IngredientDie>` and `fromElements<Zone>` in the same file.
findings.whyConcern: When three methods do nearly-the-same thing with undocumented behavioral deltas, there is no discoverable 'correct' choice. The inconsistent usage across the reference games (the very examples agents are told to study) proves even the authors don't apply a consistent rule.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An agent picks one of the three semi-randomly; if it picks the one whose value-encoding doesn't match what the custom UI sends, it gets a runtime resolution mismatch rather than a compile error. The reference games teach contradictory patterns, so few-shot imitation produces inconsistent code.
findings.fixPaths: Collapse to a single obvious pair: `chooseElement` (one) / `chooseElements` (many), unify value encoding (always element IDs), and either remove `fromElements` or make it an alias documented as deprecated-and-removed. Update the reference games to the single canonical form. Files: src/engine/action/action-builder.ts, example games.
findings.verifierReasoning: Verified all cited lines in /Users/jtsmith/BoardSmith/src/engine/action/action-builder.ts. The three methods exist (chooseFrom :153, chooseElement :254, fromElements :323) and fromElements single-select does collapse to the same internal type:'element' as chooseElement (lines 372-409 vs 281-297). So there IS real API-surface overlap and mixed naming conventions (choose*/from*/enter*, singular vs plural) — a legitimate minor DX nit. However the finding is substantially overstated: (1) The claimed 'subtle value encoding differences' between chooseElement and fromElements do not exist — both emit type:'element' and share the same resolution path; the finding inferred a delta from one JSDoc phrase. (2) The methods ARE documented with explicit guidance: chooseElement JSDoc (:220) says board-click selection 'For choices shown as buttons/dropdowns, prefer fromElements instead,' and fromElements cross-references back — contradicting 'no discoverable correct choice' / 'undocumented behavioral deltas.' (3) Game-usage evidence is factually wrong: polyhedral-potions uses only chooseElement (3x), zero fromElements — it does not mix with fromElements<Zone>; go-fish has neither method. Cribbage's mix (chooseElement single click-to-play at :68 vs fromElements multiSelect discard at :17) actually follows the documented guidance. The underlying observation is real but minor; the medium severity and most of the supporting evidence are exaggerated/incorrect.

findings.title: loop() has no default maxIterations (warn-only) while repeat()/eachPlayer() default to 100 — inconsistent safety
findings.evidence: `loop()` passes `maxIterations` straight through with no default and only emits a dev-only console warning when omitted: `src/engine/flow/builders.ts:95-104` (`if (config.maxIterations === undefined) { ... devWarn(...) }`) then `:113` `maxIterations: config.maxIterations` (i.e. `undefined`). The warning is suppressed in production (`src/utils/dev.ts:34-37` gate `devWarn`). By contrast `repeat()` (`:524 maxIterations: config.maxIterations ?? 100`) and `eachPlayer()` (`:606 ... ?? 100`) silently default to 100. The only real backstop for `loop()` is the engine-wide `DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS = 10000` (`src/engine/flow/engine.ts:28`).
findings.whyConcern: Same family of builders, three different safety behaviors. An agent learns 'loops need maxIterations or you get a warning' (per MEMORY.md) from `loop`, then is surprised `repeat`/`eachPlayer` silently cap at 100 — which can prematurely truncate a legitimately long game without an error.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: Footgun in both directions: a forgotten `loop` maxIterations spins to 10000 iterations before erroring (slow tests, the documented test warning), while a `repeat`/`eachPlayer` that legitimately needs >100 iterations silently stops with no signal. Inconsistency means the agent can't form one correct mental model.
findings.fixPaths: Make the three consistent: either all default to the same generous safety cap with a loud error on hit, or all require maxIterations explicitly (no silent default). Prefer requiring it where unbounded (`loop`) and erroring (not warning) when missing. Files: src/engine/flow/builders.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: The finding's core evidence is misattributed. It claims repeat() defaults maxIterations to 100 at builders.ts:524 and eachPlayer() at :606. Verified: line 524 is turnLoop() (defined :502) and line 606 is stateAwareLoop() (defined :568). The real repeat() (builders.ts:127-135) has no maxIterations and no 100 default — it iterates exactly `times`. The real eachPlayer() (builders.ts:147-164) has no maxIterations at all; it produces an 'each-player' node bounded by the player list. Therefore the claimed danger ("repeat/eachPlayer silently cap at 100 and prematurely truncate a long game") does not exist for either builder. The only true facts are: loop() warns dev-only (builders.ts:96-106; devWarn gated by isDevMode in dev.ts:58-62) and passes undefined through to the engine backstop DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS=10000 (engine.ts:29,1020), while turnLoop/stateAwareLoop default to 100 (builders.ts:524,606). That is a minor, plausibly intentional difference in defaults between the low-level loop and its higher-level turn wrappers — not the loop/repeat/eachPlayer inconsistency described, and not a defect. The problem as stated is not present in the code.

findings.title: boardsmith validate's sandbox scan only covers src/rules — Math.random/fetch/timers in src/ui or helpers pass silently
findings.evidence: `validateSecurity` hardcodes `const rulesDir = join(cwd, 'src/rules')` (`src/cli/commands/validate.ts:179-185`) and scans only that directory for the forbidden patterns (`:189-198` fetch/XHR/fs/setTimeout/setInterval/Date.now/Math.random/eval/Function). Game logic, however, routinely lives outside src/rules — custom UI components in `src/ui/` (the scaffold generates `src/ui/components/GameTable.vue`, `src/cli/lib/project-scaffold.ts:481`) and shared helper modules. Since the eslint-plugin (which would catch these everywhere) isn't wired in (see separate finding), this regex scan is the only active determinism guard, and it's blind outside src/rules.
findings.whyConcern: Determinism violations anywhere reachable from game state break replays, undo, AI cloning, and multiplayer — not just those in src/rules. A `Math.random()` in a UI helper that feeds a board ref or a derived value is just as corrupting but invisible to validate.
findings.pitOfSuccessImpact: An agent that factors a helper out of src/rules, or computes something nondeterministic in a Vue component, gets a green `validate` and a game that desyncs in multiplayer or produces non-reproducible replays — a silent, hard-to-trace failure that the tool implies it checks for.
findings.fixPaths: Scan all source under src/ (excluding node_modules/dist) for the forbidden patterns, or better, delegate to the AST eslint-plugin once it's wired in. File: src/cli/commands/validate.ts.
findings.verifierReasoning: The literal scope claim is accurate: validateSecurity hardcodes `const rulesDir = join(cwd, 'src/rules')` (validate.ts:180) and the only scan invocation is `scanDir(rulesDir)` (validate.ts:239); src/ui (scaffolded at project-scaffold.ts:481) is never scanned. However, the finding's central justification is wrong. Determinism is only required inside the engine sandbox — the rules bundle that the executor runs (dist/rules/rules.js, see zip.ts:27). UI code runs in the browser, only dispatches actions, and is NOT part of the deterministic replay/undo/AI-clone path; it legitimately needs the flagged APIs. BoardSmith's own UI uses setTimeout (useActionAnimations.ts:545) and Date.now (useActionController.ts:544-570) for animations/timing. Scanning src/ui for setTimeout/Math.random/Date.now would be incorrect and produce massive false positives. The claim that 'Math.random in a UI helper feeds a board ref' is false — game state is mutated only by the engine via actions. The one genuine but narrow gap is a helper module imported into rules yet living outside src/rules; that escapes the scan. But the scaffold creates nothing outside src/rules and src/ui, so it requires deliberate non-idiomatic structuring, and the better fix would be scanning the built rules bundle rather than adding src/ui. Real scope observation, but the determinism-corruption impact is largely invalid, so severity is low.
